coredata.dc:title: Sensitive fluorescence sensor for isoliquiritigenin determination based on N-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Yi
Zhao, Ziyi
Wu, Hui
coredata.dc:description: Fluorescence nanosensor based on carbon dots (CDs) is an up-and-coming platform for precise determination of isoliquiritigenin (ISO), ensuring human health and environmental safety. Herein, biomass nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) were assembled through a one-pot hydrothermal platform with lemon peel and melamine as precursors, which could be developed as distinguished sensors to detect ISO. The structure and optical properties of BN-CDs were explored in detail through some analytical methods. The BN-CDs indicated excellent UV resistance performance and biocompatibility. Moreover, after the addition of ISO, the blue fluorescence could be significantly quenched, and an eminent linear relationship was acquired between ln(F0/F) and ISO concentrations. The fitting equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.054[ISO] + 0.0085 (R2 = 0.9978) with the concentration range of 0–50 μM. 0.056 μM of detection limit was obtained based on the 3b/k. This nanosensor was successfully carried out in real samples with satisfactory recoveries of 93.3–103.9 %. This research offered a theoretical foundation towards ISO determination via blue-emitting CDs and indicated huge application potential.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Biomass carbon dots
Lemon peel
Fluorescence sensing
Quenching effect
Isoliquiritigenin

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ISO in real samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM image (A) and particle size distribution (B) of BN-CDs.
XPS full (A), C1s (B), N1s (C) and O1s spectrum (D) of BN-CDs.
UV–vis absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of BN-CDs (A); Fluorescence emission spectra of BN-CDs under different excitation wavelengths (B).

(A) Fluorescence emission signals of BN-CDs in the presence of changing amounts of ISO; (B) Fitting plot acquired by the addition of ISO to BN-CDs (inset: fluorescence color of BN-CDs solution in the presence of various ISO concentration).

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of ISO, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of BN-CDs; (B) Fluorescence lifetime curve of BN-CDs before and after adding ISO; (C) UV–vis absorption spectra of BN-CDs, ISO and BN-CDs + ISO; (D) Stern-Volmer plot acquired by the addition of ISO to BN-CDs at different temperatures.
Schematic illustration of BN-CDs for the synthesis route and ISO detection.
Comparison of methods for detection ISO.
Sensing of ISO in actual samples using this fluorescence and UV–vis method.

head.ce:title: Sensitive fluorescence sensor for isoliquiritigenin determination based on N-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang 441053 Hubei Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang 441053, Hubei Province, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Longzhong Laboratory, Xiangyang 441000 Hubei, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Longzhong Laboratory, Xiangyang 441000, Hubei, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fluorescence nanosensor based on carbon dots (CDs) is an up-and-coming platform for precise determination of isoliquiritigenin (ISO), ensuring human health and environmental safety. Herein, biomass nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) were assembled through a one-pot hydrothermal platform with lemon peel and melamine as precursors, which could be developed as distinguished sensors to detect ISO. The structure and optical properties of BN-CDs were explored in detail through some analytical methods. The BN-CDs indicated excellent UV resistance performance and biocompatibility. Moreover, after the addition of ISO, the blue fluorescence could be significantly quenched, and an eminent linear relationship was acquired between ln(F0/F) and ISO concentrations. The fitting equation was ln(F0/F) = 0.054[ISO] + 0.0085 (R2 = 0.9978) with the concentration range of 0–50 μM. 0.056 μM of detection limit was obtained based on the 3b/k. This nanosensor was successfully carried out in real samples with satisfactory recoveries of 93.3–103.9 %. This research offered a theoretical foundation towards ISO determination via blue-emitting CDs and indicated huge application potential.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Isoliquiritigenin (ISO, Fig. S1) is a natural flavonoid compound isolated from the root of Glycyrrhiza, with the chemical name of 4,2′,4′-trihydroxychalcone (molecular formula: C15H12O4) [1]. As one of the main active ingredients of Glycyrrhiza uralensis, ISO has attracted much attention in some fields because of its unique chemical structure and wide range of biological activities, such as medicine, food and cosmetics [2]. Moreover, ISO can be used as anti-tumor (as an adjuvant chemotherapy drug, reducing the toxic and side effects of traditional chemotherapy), anti-inflammatory (treating chronic inflammatory diseases), and skin repair drugs (promoting wound healing and inhibiting UV induced skin photoaging) [3,4]. As is known to all, excessive use of ISO will cause serious harm to human health at multiple levels, such as rash, pruritus, nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. Hence, there is a pressing demand to exploit several sensitive strategies for ISO detection [5]. Presently, there are only a few analytical platforms for determining ISO, such as capillary electrophoresis (CE) [6], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [7]. These traditional methods face some problems, such as cumbersome steps, time consuming, high equipment, and labor costs [8]. Fluorescence detection method is a commonly used analytical technology to detect and analyze. It has advantages of high sensitivity, good selectivity, simple operation and been widely used in biomedicine, environmental monitoring, food safety, and other fields [9–11].

Recently, carbon dots (CDs) have gained huge interest owing to their distinctive characteristics, such as preferable photostability, beneficial biocompatibility, adjustable fluorescence behavior, salt resistance, lower virulence, wide range of raw materials, and rich preparation methods [12,13]. To date, some methods have been conducted for the development of CDs, such as hydrothermal, microwave, pyrolysis, chemical oxidation, combustion, arc discharge, and acid stripping strategy [14]. Among them, hydrothermal strategy is one of the most widely employed methods in high-temperature and high-pressure closed environments, which uses solvents such as water and ethanol to promote the dehydration and carbonization of precursors, resulting in high purity and good uniformity of the products [15]. Given these excellent characteristics, CDs have been popularly utilized in some fields, such as chemical sensing, targeted drug carriers, in vivo imaging, anti-fake, and catalysis [16,17]. In addition, biomass, as a raw material, has multiple significant advantages, such as widely sourced and renewable, outstanding environmental friendliness and high cost-effectiveness [18]. For example, Tang et al. developed dual-emission CDs at 520 and 668 nm through black pepper, which were successfully employed to detect Fe3+. Due to the reducibility of ascorbic acid, fluorescence could be recovered, implying the good feasibility of ascorbic acid sensing [19]. The biomass viburnum awabuki leaves were used as precursors to prepare the dual-emission CDs by Xue et al. group [20]. The CDs could simultaneously detect Fe3+ and Cu2+. Moreover, the chestnut shell could also be applied as raw materials to develop the CDs. Yin et al. fabricated biomass-derived CDs based on chestnut shell, and the CDs were used for the sensing of PO4 3− with the detection limit (LOD) of 43.0 nM. The fluorescence response mechanism was assigned to the dynamic quenching effect [21]. To our knowledge, there is no information about fluorescence detection of ISO.

In this study, biomass nitrogen-doped carbon dots (BN-CDs) from lemon peels and melamine were successfully developed via the one-pot hydrothermal strategy (Scheme 1 ). The morphology, composition and structure of BN-CDs were determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and Fourier transform-infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). The as-developed BN-CDs were performed as fluorescent probes to determine ISO. Subsequently, we studied the selectivity and sensitivity tests for ISO. The fluorescence quenching mechanism was investigated through some analytical techniques. Additionally, some real sample experiments were carried out to discuss the feasibility in practical applications.

Sodium chloride (≥99.5 %), potassium chloride (≥99.5 %), magnesium chloride (≥98 %), ferric chloride (≥98 %), zinc chloride (≥98 %), sodium carbonate (≥99.5 %), sodium bicarbonate (≥99.8 %), and sodium sulfate (≥99 %) were obtained from Chengdu Cologne Chemicals Co., Ltd (China). Glutamate (≥99 %), glycine (≥99 %), methionine (≥99 %), histidine (≥99 %), proline (≥99 %), tyrosine (≥99 %), glucose (≥98 %), fructose (≥99 %), maltose (≥97 %), and lactose (≥98 %) were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Urea (≥99.5 %), isoliquiritigenin (ISO, ≥98 %), and melamine (≥99 %) were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. The lemon was obtained from supermarket. All materials were directly employed without any processing.

The morphology of BN-CDs was carried out via a FEI Tecnai F30 TEM (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) at a voltage of 200 kV. A Thermo Scientific K-Alpha XPS (USA) was employed to discuss the electronic binding energy of BN-CDs. The Rigaku/MAX2550 diffractometer with Cu Kα radiation (40 kV, 30 mA) (λ = 1.5418 nm) (Rigaku, Japan) was used to acquire X-ray diffraction (XRD) data. The vibration characteristic peaks of BN-CDs were studied via the Nicolet iS20 FT-IR (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA). The solid compression method was carried out with the recording range of 4000–400 cm−1. The fluorescence spectra were recorded on an F-7000 fluorescence spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the excitation/emission slits of 5/5 nm. The valence electron transition situation was studied using a UV-4500 ultraviolet–visible spectrometer (Hitachi, Japan) with the scanning speed of 1200 nm/min. The FLS920 spectrometer (Edinburgh, UK) was applied to record the fluorescence lifetime. The excitation wavelength of the light source was 368 nm.

The BN-CDs were developed through the green and environmentally friendly hydrothermal method. First, to remove moisture, the lemon peels were fully dried at 25 °C in an oven. The dried peels were transferred into a crusher to gain powder. Next, lemon peel powder (2 g) and melamine (0.5 g) were added into a beaker containing 30 mL ultrapure water. To acquire uniform mixture, the solution was stirred in a constant temperature mixer at 25 °C for 30 min. A Teflon autoclave (50 mL) was applied to prepare the CDs at 200 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the product was filtered via a 0.22 mm filter membrane and centrifuged with a centrifuge at 8000 rpm for 20 min. A dialysis bag (3000 Da) was used to purify the supernatant. Finally, the BN-CDs powder was obtained through freeze-drying.

The fluorescence sensing was developed via mixing of BN-CDs powder and disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid buffer solution (pH = 6). The BN-CDs concentration was 50 mg/mL (3.0 mL). For fluorescence experiments, various volumes of ISO (10 mM) were dropped into sensing solution via a micro pipette and mixed sufficiently for 1 min. Next, the mixture was transferred into a fluorescent colorimetric dish (1 cm × 1 cm). The emission spectra were obtained under the excitation wavelength of 368 nm. In selective experiment, the ISO solution was prepared by using N,N-dimethylformamide. The solutions of other reference substances were developed via ultrapure water.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ISO in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The Yili milk samples were obtained from the supermarket and diluted with disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid buffer solution (pH = 6) to a volume percentage content of 10 %. The tap water samples were acquired from our laboratory and filtered through a 0.22 mm filter membrane to remove the potential insoluble suspended particles. The processed water samples were diluted with buffer solutions. Then, the standard ISO solution was added into these real samples to discuss the practical applicability of BN-CDs.

The fluorescence quantum yield (QYS) of the BN-CDs was measured using a comparative method with quinine sulfate (QYR = 54 % in 0.1 M H2SO4) as the reference standard. The absorption intensities of both the BN-CDs sample and the reference solution were measured at 368 nm, followed by recording their emission spectra under the corresponding excitation wavelength. The QYS was calculated using the following equation:
Q Y S = Q Y R ×
I S
I R
×
A R
A S
×
n
S
2
n
R
2
Additionally, I denoted the integrated emission intensity, A was the absorbance, and n was the refractive index. Finally, QYS = 67 %.

The structure of BN-CDs was discussed via TEM, and the corresponding image was indicated in Fig. 1 A. The appearance of BN-CDs presented a spherical shape. According to statistical principles, the particle size distribution range were 3.3–3.7 nm with an average value of 3.5 ± 0.05 nm (Fig. 1B). The FT-IR analysis was employed to discuss the functional groups. As hinted in Fig. S2A, a series of stretching vibration characteristic peaks were recorded, such as 3393 cm−1 (O–H), 3320 cm−1 (N–H), 1600 cm−1 (C=O), and 1314 cm−1 (C-N) [22–24]. The peak at 1490 cm−1 was assigned to the bending vibration of O–H bond [25]. The deformation vibration characteristic peak of C–H bond was located at 839 cm−1 [26]. The presence of oxygen-containing functional groups indicated the outstanding water solubility, which was an important foundation for widespread application. The X-ray diffractogram (XRD) of BN-CDs displayed a broad peak at 22◦ (Fig. S2B), a characteristic carbonaceous material, implying that the BN-CDs were amorphous and highly disordered. Additionally, the element composition and valence state were further tested on the XPS characterization. It could be acquired from XPS full feature spectrum (Fig. 2 A), three peaks located at 285.2, 399.8, and 531.5 eV were assigned to the C1s, N1s, and O1s, respectively [27]. The element fraction was also recorded, such as C (65.4 %), N (15.7 %), and O (18.9 %). Next, the XPS full spectrum was processed by peak splitting. In C1s single XPS spectrum (Fig. 2B), three peaks were plainly indicated including 284.8 eV (C–C bond), 285.3 eV (C-N bond), and 288.2 eV (C=O bond) [28,29]. The N1s XPS spectrum in Fig. 2C hinted a peak at 399.4 eV, suggesting the presence of pyrrole-N [30]. Three oxygen states were observed in O1s XPS single spectrum (Fig. 2D), such as 531.2 eV (C-O bond), 532.0 eV (C=O bond), and 534.6 eV (COOH bond) [31,32]. The results of FT-IR analysis were well verified by XPS characterization. Both FT-IR and XPS analysis indicated that nitrogen was successfully doped into the as-prepared CDs.

Due to the fluorescence analysis function of BN-CDs in biological fluids, the optical performances were further studied in disodium hydrogen phosphate-citric acid buffer solution (pH = 6). The UV–vis absorption spectrum of BN-CDs was displayed in Fig. 3 A. Two peaks at 229 and 333 nm indicated the existence of π-π* (C=C bond) and n-π* (C=O bond) energy level transition [33]. The optimal emission wavelength was 438 nm at the excitation wavelength of 368 nm. Simultaneously, the colorless BN-CDs solution suggested blue fluorescence under ultraviolet light (365 nm, inset Fig. 3A). Furthermore, we discussed the fluorescence emission wavelength under different excitation wavelengths. As shown in Fig. 3B, the emission peak remained fixed as excitation wavelength varied from 340 to 390 nm (interval 10 nm), implying the presence of excitation-independent features. The characteristic was assigned to the particle size uniformity and surface defects [34,35]. Moreover, it was noteworthy that stability was a very important factor for probes in sensing and imaging fields. Thus, the fluorescence emission intensities were recorded under different test conditions. From Fig. S3A, when the NaCl concentration was up to 0.05 M, the intensity of BN-CDs maintained a high standard, suggesting that the BN-CDs had strong anti-ionic strength effect. After heating at 65 °C for 15.0 min, weak fluorescence changes were found (reduction rate of 6.6 %), implying the application possibility at higher temperatures (Fig. S3B). In addition, a weak quenching phenomenon (0.53 %) was also recorded ultraviolet irradiation for 3.0 min at 25 °C, hinting the excellent photostability (Fig. S3C). As shown in Fig. S3D, BN-CDs still maintained strong fluorescence after the storage of 15 d. To sum up, the BN-CDs suggested eminent stability even in different radical environments, ensuring the potentiality in actual applications.

To explore the specificity of this sensing system for ISO, the emission data were recorded with the addition of some other analytes, such as metal ions (Na+, K+, Mg2+, Fe3+, and Zn2+), non-metallic ions (CO3 2−, HCO3 −, and SO4 2−) and biomolecules (glutamate, glycine, methionine, histidine, proline, tyrosine, glucose, fructose, maltose, lactose, and urea). Their concentrations were all 100 μM. It could be evidently suggested from Fig. S5A, other analytes displayed negligible fluorescence variations, while significant quenching phenomenon was observed in the presence of ISO (50 μM). Additionally, the fluorescence photos of sensing solution with addition of these analytes were under UV lamp (365 nm). From Fig. S5C, except for ISO, the solutions still maintained strong blue fluorescence in the presence of controls, indicating this developed probe held an outstanding specificity for ISO, and the visual analysis could also be implemented under UV lamp (365 nm). Subsequently, we discussed the interference of other analytes to ISO. As displayed in Fig. S5B, when the ISO was added into the sensing system containing other controls, the fluorescence intensity also decreased, indicating that this nanosensor suggested prominent specificity and good anti-interference ability for the measurement of ISO.

Based on previous analysis, BN-CDs had some active functional groups (amino, hydroxyl, carboxyl, and carbonyl), which could be the binding site of analyte. Some analytical data were used to discuss the detection mechanism including fluorescence spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and fluorescence lifetime. To date, detection mechanisms mainly had Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), inner filter effect (IFE), static and dynamic quenching effect [36]. First, we discussed the possibility of FRET or IFE. As suggested in Fig. 5 A, the UV–vis absorption spectrum of ISO could significantly overlap with the excitation spectrum of BN-CDs, implying the existence of FRET and IFE [37–42]. As is known to all, the presence of FRET can result in fluorescence lifetime reduction, while negligible changes can be observed for IFE [43]. Thus, the fluorescence lifetimes of BN-CDs and BN-CDs + ISO were recorded. Based on the formula τ=(B1t1 2 + B2t2 2)/(B1t + B2t2), the average lifetimes were 1.48 ns (BN-CDs) and 1.40 ns (BN-CDs + ISO) (Fig. 5B). The slight change indicated that the detection mechanism should be IFE [44]. The negligible change also speculated the existence of static quenching effect [45]. Additionally, the UV–vis absorption spectra difference of BN-CDs, ISO and BN-CDs + ISO were discussed. As exhibited in Fig. 5C, after the addition of ISO, UV–vis absorption spectrum of BN-CDs displayed an obvious red shift from 371 nm to 396 nm, indicating the occurrence of non-fluorescent ground⁃state complexes [46]. This result suggested the presence of static quenching effect [47]. Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = K SV[Q]) was used to continuously study static quenching at different temperatures. When the temperature varied from 25 to 45 °C, the K SV value gradually decreased (Fig. 5D), implying that static quenching was one of the main mechanisms [48–51]. To sum up, IFE and static quenching were coexisting quenching mechanisms.

Due to the outstanding specificity and rapid responsiveness of BN-CDs for the determination of ISO, this probe system was used to quantitatively detect ISO in Yili milk and tap water samples. The detection results of spiked ISO amounts (15.0, 25.0 and 35.0 μM) were suggested in Table 2 . The detected concentrations were acquired from the fluorescence intensity recording and fitting linear equation (ln(F0/F = 0.054[ISO] + 0.0085)). All values were the average of three measurements. Satisfied recoveries were acquired in Yili milk (93.3–102.6 %) and tap water samples (95.6–103.9 %). The relative standard deviation (RSD) was located at the range of 1.47–3.78 %. The results of the proposed method were in agreement with the UV–vis method with recoveries ranging from 96.8 % to 105.1 % and RSD was < 3.39 %. These recovery tests implied that this probe had huge potential for the detection of ISO in real samples.

Herein, the lemon peels and melamine were used as precursors to prepare bio-based blue-emitting BN-CDs through facile solvothermal strategy. The as-developed BN-CDs hinted good water-solubility and outstanding stability under various conditions. The fluorescent detection tests displayed that this BN-CDs-based nanosensor suggested outstanding specificity and rapid responsiveness for the determination of ISO with a wide linear range of 0–50 μM (LOD of 0.056 μM). The static quenching effect and IFE were the coexisting fluorescence quenching mechanisms. Moreover, this probe system could be applied to quantitatively determine ISO in Yili milk and tap water samples. Satisfied recoveries were acquired in Yili milk (93.3–102.6 %) and tap water samples (95.6–103.9 %). The as-established system exhibited the superiorities of facile process, time saving, low consumption, and rapid responsiveness, suggesting a huge potential method for building fluorescence probes for the detection of various analytes.
Yi Zhang: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Conceptualization. Ziyi Zhao, Hui Wu: Validation, Software, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the Student Innovation Training Program (No.202110519017) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2025.126554.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
